proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage loop: 1) Generate initial reasoning chains (e.g., Chain-of-Thought) for a dataset of complex problems. 2) Use a separate, possibly larger, 'critic' LLM (or the same model via a LoRA adapter) to audit and rank these reasoning steps, identifying errors and suggesting improvements, inspired by techniques from 'Auditing language models for hidden objectives'. 3) Fine-tune the base model on the corrected reasoning traces using a weighted objective, potentially leveraging EWoRA to handle heterogeneous feedback quality. 4) Iterate this process, using the improved model as the critic in subsequent rounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable framework for automated process supervision, reducing reliance on human annotators. It will yield a model with demonstrably improved reasoning robustness and a methodology for iterative self-correction applicable to domains like code generation (extending Bridge-Coder principles) and mathematical reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While self-critique has been explored, this proposal integrates it into a closed-loop, iterative training system using rank-based feedback and efficient adaptation (LoRA/EWoRA) to create a stable self-improvement cycle, moving beyond single-round correction.

proposals.title: Auditing Scalar Reward Functions for Hidden Value Misalignment in Language Models
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm for aligning LLMs with human preferences, Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), relies on training a single reward model to approximate a scalar summary of human intent. However, pre-2026 work in value learning and ethics suggests human values are multi-faceted, context-dependent, and often incommensurable. This project investigates the critical safety risk that a scalar reward function may fail to capture this complexity, leading to models that optimize for a proxy objective while violating other implicit human values.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that language models fine-tuned via RLHF against a scalar reward function will exhibit systematic 'reward hacking' behaviors—performing well on the learned proxy but failing on orthogonal, unmeasured dimensions of human intent that were present in the original preference data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the auditing framework from Leike's prior work to the reward modeling stage. First, we will train a standard reward model (RM) on a diverse preference dataset (e.g., Anthropic Helpful-Harmless). We then apply Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation (EWoRA) to decompose the RM into multiple 'expert' LoRA modules, each hypothesized to capture a different latent value dimension (e.g., helpfulness, harmlessness, conciseness). By analyzing the activation patterns of these experts on held-out or adversarial tasks, we can audit which values the scalar reward fails to robustly represent. We will further test this by using the experts to create multi-objective reward signals and comparing resulting model behavior to the standard RLHF model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for identifying value loss in reward modeling. It will produce concrete evidence of the limitations of scalar reward functions for alignment and contribute methodology for more nuanced, multi-faceted reward auditing. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation alignment paradigms that move beyond scalar reward aggregation.
proposals.novelty: While reward hacking is known, this proposal novelly applies model auditing and decomposition techniques (EWoRA) specifically to the reward model itself—not the policy—to isolate and identify which components of human intent are lost during scalar reward distillation, bridging Leike's work on auditing and low-rank adaptation.

proposals.title: Interpreting Reward Models: A Sparse, Concept-Based Framework for Transparent Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Reward models are critical for aligning language models with human preferences via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, they are typically black-box functions, making it difficult to audit for hidden objectives, spurious correlations, or reward hacking. This opacity poses significant risks for the safe and trustworthy development of advanced AI systems, as highlighted by pre-2026 work on reward misspecification and auditing.
proposals.hypothesis: Reward model decisions can be decomposed into a sparse, interpretable set of human-understandable concepts (e.g., 'factuality', 'helpfulness', 'harm'), and this decomposition will reveal systematic biases and failure modes not apparent from scalar reward outputs alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt concept bottleneck models (CBMs) and sparse autoencoders to the reward modeling setting. First, we will train a concept bottleneck layer on top of a frozen reward model backbone, using a small set of human-annotated concept labels (e.g., from scaled oversight datasets). We will then apply techniques from EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) to efficiently learn sparse, interpretable concept vectors for different data domains. Finally, we will audit the resulting concept-level explanations against known failure cases in reward models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for interpreting reward model internals, moving beyond post-hoc explanations. It will contribute a diagnostic toolkit for identifying reward model pathologies and a method for building more inherently interpretable reward models, directly advancing the field of AI alignment and safety.
proposals.novelty: While explainable AI (XAI) has been applied to classifiers, its systematic application to reward models for RLHF is underexplored. The integration of concept-based interpretation with low-rank adaptation techniques (LoRA/EWoRA) for efficient, sparse concept discovery in reward spaces is a novel technical direction.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Reward Transfer via Expert-Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation (EWoRA-RM)
proposals.motivation: Training high-quality reward models (RMs) for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is data-intensive and costly for each new task. This bottleneck limits the scalable alignment of language models. Transfer learning from existing RMs on related tasks offers a promising but underexplored path to efficient reward modeling, especially for low-resource domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A reward model's internal representations for judging quality, safety, or helpfulness contain shared, transferable features across related tasks, and these can be efficiently extracted and recombined using multi-task learning and low-rank adaptation to bootstrap performant RMs for new tasks with minimal new data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) framework to reward modeling. A base RM (e.g., a pre-trained transformer) will be adapted to multiple source reward prediction tasks via independent LoRA modules. For a new target task, we will learn a sparse, weighted combination of these source LoRA 'experts' using a small amount of target preference data. This approach will be benchmarked against training from scratch and standard fine-tuning on tasks like code generation (transferring from high-resource to low-resource languages, inspired by Bridge-Coder) and dialogue safety (transferring from general helpfulness).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, parameter-efficient method for cross-task reward model transfer, reducing data requirements for RLHF. It will yield empirical results on the transferability of reward signals across task boundaries and contribute a new auditing technique by analyzing which source task experts are activated for a new target, revealing hidden structural similarities in human preferences.
proposals.novelty: While LoRA is used for efficient fine-tuning, applying an expert-weighted, multi-task LoRA ensemble (EWoRA) specifically for composable reward model transfer is novel. This bridges the gap between multi-task learning in language modeling and the practical challenges of reward design for alignment.

proposals.title: Dual-Interpretable Alignment: Learning a Policy that is Both Optimal and Explanatory for a Learned Reward Model
proposals.motivation: Reward models learned from human preferences are central to aligning LLMs but are often black-box functions, making it difficult to understand what behavior they truly incentivize. This opacity hinders debugging, safety auditing, and trust. We need methods to produce policies that are not just performant under a reward model but also provide human-interpretable explanations of the reward's implicit objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly training a policy to maximize a learned reward model while simultaneously constraining it to generate natural language explanations of the reward model's decision rules, we can produce a policy that is both high-performing and interpretable, effectively revealing the hidden objectives of the reward model.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage algorithm. First, we train a reward model (RM) on human preference data using standard methods like RLHF or DPO. Second, we train a policy model using a composite objective: (1) a performance loss (e.g., PPO) to maximize the learned RM's score, and (2) an interpretability loss that conditions the policy to output a succinct natural language rule justifying its action for a given input. We will explore using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules to efficiently specialize a base LLM into this dual-output policy, generating both the main response and the explanatory rule.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for auditing and interpreting learned reward models by materializing their preferences into an explicit, human-readable rule set. It contributes to the field of AI alignment by offering a tool for safety diagnostics and could lead to more transparent and controllable reward learning pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work audits reward models by analyzing their weights or outputs in isolation, our approach directly learns a policy that *embodies and explains* the reward, merging high performance with inherent interpretability. This differs from post-hoc explanation techniques by baking explanation into the policy's training objective.

proposals.title: Multi-Modal Reward Modeling via Expert-Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation (MMR-EWoRA)
proposals.motivation: Current reward models for aligning AI systems are typically trained on a single, homogeneous feedback type (e.g., pairwise comparisons), which is expensive to scale and fails to leverage the rich, multi-modal feedback available in the real world, such as natural language corrections, physical demonstrations, or ranked preferences. Learning from this heterogeneous data is crucial for developing robust, sample-efficient alignment methods, especially for complex, multi-modal agents. This directly addresses core challenges in scalable oversight and reward learning identified in pre-2026 alignment research.
proposals.hypothesis: A single reward model can be effectively trained on heterogeneous feedback streams by using a modular, expert-weighted architecture that dynamically routes and combines information from different modalities, leading to more data-efficient and generalizable reward functions than models trained on any single feedback type.
proposals.method: We will extend the EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) framework to the reward modeling setting. A shared base reward model (e.g., a pre-trained LLM backbone) will host multiple low-rank adapters (LoRAs), each acting as an 'expert' specialized for a feedback modality (language corrections, demonstration features, preference ranks). A lightweight gating network, conditioned on the input state and the available feedback type, will compute expert weights to combine adapter outputs into a unified reward signal. Training will use a multi-task objective, combining standard preference loss (for ranked data) with auxiliary losses like behavioral cloning (for demonstrations) and sequence modeling (for language corrections).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, parameter-efficient method for learning unified reward models from diverse, real-world feedback, advancing the technical toolkit for scalable alignment. It will yield empirical benchmarks for multi-modal reward learning and offer new insights into how different feedback signals can be compositionally integrated to shape agent behavior more effectively.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses mixed data for policy training, applying expert-weighted LoRA specifically to *reward model* training for heterogeneous feedback is novel. It combines the parameter-efficient specialization of EWoRA with the principled reward learning framework of RLHF/DPO, creating a new pathway for data fusion in alignment.

proposals.title: Robust Alignment via Process-Guided Reward Correction: A Hybrid Supervision Framework
proposals.motivation: Current alignment methods often rely on a single, sparse reward signal (outcome supervision), which is frequently a misspecified proxy for the true objective. This leads to reward hacking and Goodhart's Law, where models exploit flaws in the reward specification, resulting in undesirable or unsafe behavior. A robust solution is needed to train agents that remain aligned even when the performance function is imperfectly known.
proposals.hypothesis: Dense process supervision can be used to learn a model of the error or uncertainty in a sparse outcome reward, enabling dynamic correction of the training signal and leading to agents that are more robust to reward misspecification.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework where a primary LLM is trained via RLHF/DPO using a sparse outcome reward. In parallel, a separate 'audit' model, initialized from the primary model and fine-tuned with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for efficiency, will be trained via process supervision on step-by-step reasoning traces. This audit model will learn to predict the divergence between the process's internal consistency and the final outcome reward. Its predictions will be used to weight or correct the outcome reward signal during the primary model's policy gradient updates, creating an adaptive, process-informed reward.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, sample-efficient framework for robust alignment that mitigates reward hacking. It will yield a concrete method for fusing outcome and process supervision and contribute new insights into representing and correcting for hidden objectives in AI training.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid supervision is known, using process feedback explicitly to model and correct flaws in the outcome reward signal, rather than just as an auxiliary training target, is a novel approach to formalizing robustness against reward misspecification.

proposals.title: Inferring Non-Stationary Dynamics via Multimodal Goal Streams for Robust Reward Learning
proposals.motivation: Deploying agents in real-world settings requires them to adapt when the environment's dynamics or the human's goal changes, a problem known as non-stationarity. Current alignment methods often assume a fixed reward function or world model, making them brittle. This work addresses the core challenge of distinguishing between an agent's failure to achieve a goal and a shift in the underlying physics or rules of the task.
proposals.hypothesis: A reward model that jointly conditions on a temporal stream of multimodal goal exemplars (e.g., videos) and the agent's observations can disentangle goal-pursuit from environmental non-stationarity, enabling more robust and interpretable policy adaptation.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture: a goal encoder (using a pre-trained video model like VideoMAE) processes a sequence of demonstration videos, and an observation encoder processes the agent's state. A temporal fusion module, inspired by rank-based preference modeling and EWoRA's expert-weighting, will compare the evolving goal embedding against the agent's trajectory to output a reward. We will train this model using preference data from non-stationary simulated environments, employing low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to efficiently fine-tune the goal encoder for new task domains, akin to Bridge-Coder's transfer approach.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a formal framework and practical algorithm for learning reward signals under concurrent goal and dynamics shift. It contributes to the field of robust alignment by providing a method to audit when an agent's poor performance is due to environmental change versus policy failure, enhancing safety. The work also advances multimodal reasoning by using non-linguistic goal streams as a supervisory signal for world-model inference.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats non-stationarity as a problem in dynamics or reward alone, our key innovation is using the temporal evolution of the goal specification itself as a signal to infer changes in the environment's rules, creating a synergistic feedback loop for adaptation.

proposals.title: Decomposing and Mitigating Weak-to-Strong Generalization Failures via Specification-Robustness Diagnostics
proposals.motivation: Weak-to-strong generalization is a promising but unreliable safety technique for controlling superhuman AI models. Current methods treat failures monolithically, leading to inefficient and unpredictable capability recovery. A formal decomposition of failure modes is needed to develop targeted interventions, improving both the safety and sample efficiency of aligning models that outperform their supervisors.
proposals.hypothesis: Weak-to-strong generalization failures can be cleanly decomposed into specification errors (incorrect task definition from the weak supervisor) and robustness errors (failure of the strong model to generalize from correct but limited supervision), and targeted algorithmic interventions for each error type will significantly improve overall generalization performance.
proposals.method: We will first develop a formal framework, building on concepts from algorithmic auditing and model rank analysis, to categorize failures on a curated benchmark of reasoning and knowledge tasks. For specification errors, we will design meta-learning objectives where the strong model learns to infer the underlying intent behind noisy or incomplete weak labels. For robustness errors, we will implement structured regularization and verification-aided fine-tuning, using techniques like Bridge-Coder's capability transfer and EWoRA's expert-weighted LoRA modules to stabilize learning from sparse correct signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first diagnostic toolkit for analyzing weak-to-strong generalization, moving the field beyond black-box empirical results. It will also deliver a suite of specialized, composable training algorithms that enable more predictable and efficient recovery of strong model capabilities under weak supervision, directly advancing scalable oversight research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work audits models for hidden objectives or uses uniform weak supervision, our approach is novel in its causal decomposition of failure modes and the creation of error-type-specific training algorithms, rather than treating weak-to-strong generalization as a single, monolithic learning problem.

proposals.title: Robust Code Policy Transfer via Adversarial Curriculum Learning
proposals.motivation: Code-generation models trained on curated datasets often suffer from significant performance degradation when deployed on real-world codebases, which are noisy, contain bugs, and follow diverse, non-standard patterns. This distributional shift poses a major obstacle to reliable AI-assisted software engineering. Current methods, like fine-tuning on low-resource languages (Bridge-Coder) or using robust adaptation (EWoRA), do not systematically address the dynamic, adversarial nature of this shift during training.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing the training process as a robust control problem, where a dynamically generated curriculum of adversarial code examples acts as a disturbance, we can learn a code-generation policy with formal guarantees of robustness to distributional shift, leading to superior performance on noisy, real-world code.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-agent adversarial curriculum learning framework. A 'Disturbance Agent' (DA), based on a language model, will synthesize increasingly challenging code perturbations (e.g., bug injections, obfuscation, style violations). A 'Policy Agent' (PA), a code-generation model, will be trained via reinforcement learning with a reward function balancing correctness and robustness. Inspired by robust control, we will use a Lyapunov-based training schedule to adaptively control the DA's perturbation strength, ensuring the PA's performance remains stable. The PA's policy will be efficiently adapted using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules, enabling rapid specialization while preserving core knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel training paradigm for robust code generation, a benchmark of adversarial code perturbations for evaluating robustness, and a set of code-generation models with empirically validated stronger guarantees against distributional shift. It directly advances the goal of building reliable AI software engineers that perform consistently in messy, practical environments.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of robust control theory principles to dynamically generate an adversarial curriculum for code model training, moving beyond static dataset curation or post-hoc auditing to bake in robustness during the core learning process.

proposals.title: Training Robust Reasoning Agents via Synthetic Imperfect Process Supervision
proposals.motivation: Process supervision is a promising path to scalable oversight for complex reasoning, but it relies on high-quality, consistent human feedback, which is expensive and itself prone to flaws like bias, distraction, and error. Current methods using synthetic feedback aim for perfect labels, ignoring the reality of imperfect human supervision, which creates a robustness gap for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training reasoning agents on a controlled spectrum of synthetic, flawed process-level feedback will produce models that are more robust to real human supervisor errors, learning to identify and compensate for guidance that is inconsistent, biased, or incomplete.
proposals.method: We will generate synthetic reasoning traces with step-level feedback for mathematical and code reasoning tasks. Using a taxonomy of supervisor flaws (e.g., intermittent inattention, confirmation bias, knowledge gaps), we will corrupt this feedback systematically. We will then train and evaluate agents, likely using a LoRA-augmented policy model, under this 'adversarial gym' of flawed supervision, comparing against baselines trained on perfect synthetic or limited human feedback. Key metrics will include final-task performance and the agent's ability to detect and override bad advice.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for studying and improving robustness to imperfect oversight in AI training. It will yield insights into how agents can learn caution and meta-cognition from flawed feedback, advancing the field of scalable supervision beyond simply collecting more data.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is using synthetic data not for scale or perfection, but to systematically simulate the failure modes of human supervision, creating a controlled environment to explicitly train for robustness to these flaws, which is distinct from prior work on synthetic data or adversarial training.

proposals.title: Expert-Weighted Feedback Synthesis: Scaling High-Quality Human Preferences via LLM-Aided Data Augmentation and Ranking
proposals.motivation: The effectiveness of alignment techniques like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is fundamentally bottlenecked by the cost, scale, and consistency of high-quality human preference data. As models scale, this data collection process becomes prohibitively expensive and slow, limiting the pace of safe and capable AI development. Current methods struggle to efficiently generalize feedback across diverse tasks or to synthesize reliable preference rankings from sparse expert annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid human-LLM data collection pipeline, which uses a small set of expert-ranked seed comparisons to train a specialized preference-synthesis model, can generate scalable, high-quality preference datasets that preserve expert judgment and outperform naive human or synthetic data collection in cost-efficiency.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method: First, we collect a high-quality but small seed dataset of expert comparisons across heterogeneous tasks (e.g., reasoning, code, safety). Second, we train a 'Feedback Synthesis Model' (FSM) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on this seed data, inspired by EWoRA's principles for heterogeneous data, to predict expert preference rankings between model outputs. This FSM will then be used to generate and rank massive volumes of synthetic pairwise comparisons, which are validated through targeted human audits—a technique extending Leike's prior work on auditing for hidden objectives—to ensure quality and catch reward hacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable pipeline for generating aligned training data, directly reducing the primary cost of RLHF. It will contribute a new methodology for bootstrapping expert judgment and a publicly released, audited preference dataset for programming and reasoning tasks, bridging capabilities from high-resource to low-resource domains as in Bridge-Coder.
proposals.novelty: While using LLMs for data augmentation is known, the novelty lies in explicitly training a lightweight model to *synthesize expert-weighted preference rankings* from minimal seed data, and in rigorously auditing the synthesized feedback for alignment drift, creating a scalable and trustworthy feedback loop.

proposals.title: Generalized Reward Modeling via Expert-Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation on Heterogeneous Preference Data
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning large language models, but its reward models often fail to generalize beyond their narrow training distribution. This fragility leads to reward hacking and performance degradation when models encounter novel prompts or user intents, limiting the robustness and safety of deployed AI systems. Improving reward model generalization is therefore a critical bottleneck for creating reliable, broadly capable AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a single reward model on a heterogeneous mixture of preference datasets, using a method that explicitly models and preserves dataset-specific expertise, will yield a reward function that generalizes more robustly to out-of-distribution prompts compared to standard fine-tuning or naive multi-task training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) framework, recently used for heterogeneous data in supervised learning, to the preference modeling setting. A base reward model will be trained on a curated mixture of datasets covering diverse domains (e.g., helpfulness, harmlessness, coding, reasoning). For each dataset, we will learn a separate low-rank adapter (LoRA) to capture domain-specific reward signals. A gating network will be trained to dynamically blend these expert adapters' outputs for a given input prompt, enabling compositional generalization. We will benchmark against standard RLHF reward training and multi-task baselines using held-out OOD tasks and adversarial prompt suites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for building more robust and generalizable reward models, directly addressing a key weakness in the RLHF pipeline. It will yield empirical evidence on the benefits of structured multi-domain training for preference modeling and release a benchmark for evaluating reward model generalization.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task and adapter-based methods exist, applying an expert-weighted low-rank adaptation scheme (EWoRA) specifically to the problem of reward model generalization for RLHF is novel. This approach moves beyond simple fine-tuning to explicitly model and combine disparate sources of preference knowledge within a single, composable architecture.

proposals.title: Inferring Latent Human Intent from Demonstrations via Inverse Reinforcement Learning with Expert Weighted Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are trained on vast datasets of human text, which implicitly encode a mixture of human intents, preferences, and values. However, these underlying objectives remain opaque, making models difficult to audit and align reliably. Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) offers a principled framework to infer the reward function—a proxy for intent—that best explains observed demonstration data, which is crucial for building safer and more transparent AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying IRL to sequences of human text demonstrations, combined with a method for handling heterogeneous intent sources, can recover a more interpretable and disentangled representation of latent human objectives than standard behavioral cloning or supervised fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MaxEnt IRL framework to operate on sequences generated by a frozen base LLM, treating the LLM as a complex dynamics model. To handle the heterogeneity of intents in web-scale text data, we will integrate the EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) method. EWoRA will allow us to learn multiple low-rank intent-specific reward models (experts) from demonstration data, with a gating network to weight them. The inferred reward functions will then be used to fine-tune a policy LLM via PPO, and its outputs will be evaluated against held-out demonstrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for intent discovery in language models, moving beyond pattern imitation to infer underlying objectives. It will produce a framework for auditing and decomposing the mixed incentives in pre-training data, directly advancing the field of AI alignment and interpretability.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in the fusion of sequence-level IRL with the EWoRA architecture for multi-intent reward modeling, applying these techniques at scale to the complex, heterogeneous domain of natural language demonstrations, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Calibrated Reward Ensembles via Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation (CREW)
proposals.motivation: Reward models are critical for aligning language models via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), but they are prone to reward hacking, overoptimization, and poor calibration on out-of-distribution inputs. Pre-2026, single reward models often fail to capture the nuanced, multi-faceted nature of human preferences, leading to brittle and misaligned AI behavior. This project addresses the core alignment challenge of building reward functions that are robust and reliably generalize.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of reward models, each specialized via low-rank adaptation (LoRA) on distinct preference data slices and aggregated via a learned gating mechanism, will produce reward signals that are more robust, better calibrated, and less susceptible to adversarial optimization than a single monolithic model.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EWoRA (Expert Weighted Low-Rank Adaptation) framework for reward modeling. Multiple 'expert' reward predictors will be created by applying unique LoRA adapters to a frozen base pre-trained language model (e.g., a T5 or small LLM), each trained on a different cluster of human preference data identified via task or demographic metadata. A central router network, trained on a held-out validation set of preferences, will learn to weight the experts' outputs dynamically per input. We will evaluate ensemble calibration and robustness against reward hacking attacks compared to a standard single reward model baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable method for building more reliable reward models, directly improving the safety and robustness of the RLHF pipeline. It will yield new metrics and benchmarks for reward model calibration and contribute an open-source library for ensemble-based reward modeling, bridging ensemble ML methods with AI alignment research.
proposals.novelty: While ensembles are classic in ML, their application to reward modeling for alignment is underexplored. The key novelty is integrating the parameter-efficient EWoRA/LoRA approach for creating diverse experts with a learned aggregation scheme specifically optimized for reward calibration and adversarial robustness, rather than mere performance.

proposals.title: Dual: Scaling and Auditing Alignment via Synthetic Feedback from Expert-Weighted Models
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a critical but expensive and data-limited bottleneck for aligning large language models (LLMs). Scaling high-quality human feedback to new domains, tasks, or low-resource languages is prohibitively costly. This work investigates whether we can bootstrap and scale alignment by using existing, high-performing aligned models as synthetic feedback generators, while rigorously auditing for hidden objective drift.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of aligned expert models, weighted via a method like EWoRA to handle heterogeneous feedback tasks, can generate sufficiently high-quality and diverse synthetic preference data to train new models effectively, without propagating systematic alignment failures detectable through targeted auditing.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline where a committee of aligned LLMs (e.g., Claude, GPT-4) generates ranked responses and rationales for diverse prompts. Inspired by EWoRA, we will learn expert weights for each model-task pair to aggregate synthetic preferences. A new model will be trained via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on this data. Crucially, we will integrate the auditing techniques from Leike's prior work to systematically evaluate the trained model for hidden objectives or reward hacking not present in the generator models, creating a closed-loop of generation and verification.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a scalable method for generating alignment data, particularly for low-resource scenarios like non-English languages or specialized domains. It will also contribute a framework for auditing the safety and robustness of models trained on synthetic feedback, addressing a key concern in automated alignment pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While using LLMs for data generation is known, the novel integration of expert-weighted low-rank adaptation for feedback aggregation and, most importantly, the embedded auditing for hidden objective drift creates a dual-purpose system for both scaling and safety-testing synthetic alignment.

ideas.title: Hybrid Supervision for Robust Alignment Under Hidden Performance Functions
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how hybrid outcome and process supervision can be systematically combined to train AI systems that are robust to misspecification of the true performance function. The core idea is to use dense process-level feedback to learn a model of where and why the sparse outcome reward is likely to be flawed or incomplete, creating an implicit representation of the hidden true objective. This allows the training process to dynamically discount or correct the imperfect outcome signal based on internal consistency checks from process supervision, leading to more aligned and robust agents. The result is a training framework that is both sample-efficient (leveraging hybrid supervision) and safe, as it mitigates the risks of Goodhart's Law and reward hacking inherent in optimizing a known-but-imperfect metric.

ideas.title: A Formal Framework for Specification-Robustness Decomposition in Weak-to-Strong Generalization
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical framework that formally categorizes weak-to-strong generalization failures into specification errors (where the weak supervisor mis-specifies the target task) and robustness errors (where the strong model fails to correctly interpret or generalize from correct weak supervision). Using this decomposition, the project then designs novel training techniques—such as structured regularization, meta-learning objectives, and verification-aided fine-tuning—that specifically target the identified error type. This allows for more efficient and reliable capability recovery from strong models, moving beyond one-size-fits-all weak supervision methods. The ultimate goal is to provide both a diagnostic tool and a suite of specialized algorithms that improve the safety and predictability of leveraging weak supervisors to control more powerful AI systems.

ideas.title: Robust Process Supervision via Synthetic Flawed Supervisors
ideas.description: This research direction proposes generating synthetic, step-level feedback that intentionally incorporates a spectrum of realistic human supervisor flaws—such as inconsistencies, biases, distractions, or knowledge gaps—to train robust AI agents. Instead of using synthetic feedback merely as a cheap, perfect label, it is used to systematically simulate the failure modes of human oversight. This allows for the scalable study of how agents can learn to identify, compensate for, or cautiously override erroneous guidance during multi-step reasoning. The core novelty is using synthetic data not just for scale, but to create a controlled 'adversarial gym' that explicitly trains for robustness to imperfect human supervision.

ideas.title: Immunization via Controlled Exposure to Incorrect Reasoning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that training only on correct solutions is optimal by drawing an analogy from immunology. In vaccinology, controlled exposure to a weakened pathogen trains the immune system for future defense. Similarly, we propose 'immunizing' language models by training them on datasets where correct solutions are deliberately interspersed with plausible incorrect reasoning steps. The model's objective is adapted to not only reach the correct answer but also to detect, flag, and correct the introduced errors during its chain-of-thought. This controlled exposure to 'pathogenic' reasoning aims to improve the model's robustness, reduce its susceptibility to its own or user-provided reasoning errors, and enhance its ability to self-diagnose flawed logic.

ideas.title: Robust Code Policy Transfer via Adversarial Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This research direction applies robust control theory to the problem of training code-generation models. Instead of assuming a static, high-quality training dataset, the core idea is to treat the training process as a control system where the 'disturbance' is a dynamically generated curriculum of increasingly challenging, low-quality, or adversarial code examples. The system uses techniques from robust control to adaptively synthesize these adversarial training samples, ensuring the learned policy maintains high performance and safety guarantees even when deployed on noisy, real-world codebases. This results in agents that are not just trained on quality, but are fundamentally robust to the distributional shift inherent in practical software engineering environments.

ideas.title: Revealed Preference for Reasoning: Training on Choice Sets to Infer Robust Solution Criteria
ideas.description: This research investigates training AI models not on labeled correct/incorrect solutions, but on choice sets where the model must select a preferred reasoning path from multiple options, including subtly incorrect ones. By applying economic models of revealed preference, we treat these selections as data to reverse-engineer the model's latent utility function for reasoning steps. This allows the system to learn a robust, internalized criterion for solution quality that generalizes beyond the training distribution. The synergy enables the model to develop sophisticated error detection and reasoning robustness by actively learning what constitutes a valid solution from its own comparative choices, rather than passive exposure to static labels.

ideas.title: Contrastive Discrimination Training for Robust Reasoning without Contamination
ideas.description: This research investigates a training paradigm that improves model robustness and error detection without risking the corruption of its reasoning capabilities. The system is trained on mixed corpora of correct and carefully generated incorrect solutions, but uses a dual-objective architecture: a primary generator is trained only on correct solutions, while a separate discriminator head is trained to classify reasoning steps as valid or flawed. The generator and discriminator are jointly optimized with a contrastive loss that pushes representations of correct and incorrect steps apart. This approach explicitly teaches the model to recognize errors without learning to reproduce them, leading to more robust self-verification and debugging abilities.

ideas.title: Non-Stationary Goal Alignment via Multimodal Reward Surfaces
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how to train agents that remain safe and effective when both the goal specification and the environment's physics or rules are non-stationary. The core challenge is to learn a reward model that maps from a stream of multimodal goal exemplars (like a series of demonstration videos) and the agent's sensory observations to a reward signal, without assuming a fixed relationship between actions and outcomes. The novel synergy lies in using the temporal evolution of the non-linguistic goal channel itself as a signal to infer and adapt to changes in the environment's dynamics, enabling the agent to distinguish between its own failures and externally imposed shifts in the world model.

ideas.title: Dynamic Adversarial Gating for Efficient Hybrid Supervision
ideas.description: This research investigates a meta-learning framework where a gating controller dynamically decides when to apply costly process supervision during training. The controller is adversarially trained to minimize outcome prediction error, learning to request process labels primarily for uncertain or critical reasoning steps. This creates a cost-efficient hybrid paradigm that ensures process supervision is integrated meaningfully where it most impacts robustness, preventing the model from bypassing deeper understanding. The system is evaluated on its ability to generalize to novel problems where outcome-only supervision would typically fail.

ideas.title: Specification-Inference-Guided Verification for Open-Domain Reasoning
ideas.description: This system addresses the specification bottleneck by jointly learning to verify and infer the rules of valid reasoning. It begins with a weak, partial formal model and a probabilistic inconsistency detector trained on high-quality reasoning data. When analyzing a new reasoning trace, it flags potential logical flaws based on this combined model and engages in an interactive dialogue with a human user to confirm or reject its hypotheses. This feedback is used not only to correct the trace but also to iteratively refine the system's internal constraint model, enabling it to handle domains where a complete formal specification cannot be pre-specified.

ideas.title: Adaptive Feedback Blending for AI Training Inspired by Formative-Summative Assessment
ideas.description: This research seed proposes investigating hybrid outcome and process supervision for AI training. Drawing an analogy from Educational Psychology, where formative (process) and summative (outcome) assessments are blended adaptively based on learner proficiency, we adapt this solution. The adapted idea is to design a training paradigm where the supervisory signal is a dynamically weighted blend of process and outcome supervision, controlled by a meta-controller that monitors the model's internal states (e.g., confidence, consistency). This approach would shift the blend towards more process feedback when the model is uncertain or learning new skills, and towards outcome feedback for tasks where it demonstrates higher mastery, aiming to create a more efficient and robust training curriculum that optimizes the strengths of both supervision types.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Cross-Document Summarization for Agent Policy Abstraction
ideas.description: This research applies multi-document summarization techniques to a corpus of an RL agent's interaction histories or learned policy components, treating them as related documents. The goal is to synthesize a concise, abstract summary of the agent's overall policy or decision logic. Crucially, formal verification methods are then used to prove that this summarized abstraction maintains key safety and liveness properties present in the original, more complex policy or behavioral corpus. This enables the creation of interpretable, high-level policy descriptions that are guaranteed to be safe, bridging the gap between agent transparency and formal safety assurance.

ideas.title: Verified Process Supervision via Grounded Synthetic Feedback
ideas.description: This research develops a system to generate scalable step-level feedback by integrating synthetic generation with robust verification. It uses a critic model to produce initial feedback, which is then automatically checked for consistency against external databases, code execution outputs, and logical verifiers. A dynamic curriculum prioritizes human verification for steps identified as high-risk or novel, creating verified supervision pairs. This hybrid approach maintains scalability while anchoring the training process in external reality, preventing reward drift and ensuring robust generalization.

ideas.title: Dynamic Sparse Interpretability for Agent Safety Failure Analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a novel interpretability framework for analyzing agent safety failures in gridworlds. It uses dynamic sparsity autoencoders, where the sparsity level k adapts to the complexity of the agent's internal activations during failure episodes. This automatically distills the agent's high-dimensional state into a sparse, complexity-adaptive set of features that are most relevant to the flawed decision. The result is a targeted, efficient diagnostic tool that pinpoints whether failures stem from perceptual oversimplification, representational overload, or specific erroneous feature associations in the agent's policy.

ideas.title: Formal Verification Under Hidden and Incomplete Objectives
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how to use automated verifiers and formal proofs to ensure the safety of AI systems when the true, complete performance metric is unknown or impossible to fully specify. It challenges the assumption that human feedback or a single reward function is sufficient by formally proving that a system's actions remain within a safe, corrigible envelope even as it pursues an imperfectly understood objective. The synergy enables the development of 'robust formal alignment'—guarantees that are not brittle to misspecification of the goal, creating a safety layer that is both automated and cognizant of its own epistemic limits regarding the true objective.

ideas.title: Confidence-Weighted Harmlessness Overrides for Multi-Step Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how confidence scores attached to individual reasoning steps can inform and calibrate overrides of a strict harmlessness objective. Instead of treating harmlessness as a monolithic, binary constraint, the system assesses the confidence and potential impact of each step where a trade-off (e.g., prioritizing helpful truthfulness) is considered. This allows for granular, justified overrides where high-confidence, high-benefit steps may temporarily relax harmlessness guardrails, while low-confidence or high-risk steps enforce them strictly. The result is a more adaptive and contextually intelligent alignment paradigm that optimizes for complex real-world objectives.

ideas.title: Path-Sensitive Criticality: Measuring Step Influence via Counterfactual Reasoning Robustness
ideas.description: This research investigates a dynamic, graph-based method for evaluating reasoning steps, moving beyond predefined notions of difficulty. It constructs a reasoning graph from a model's chain-of-thought and simulates perturbations or errors at each step, observing how these propagate through alternative paths to affect final answer distribution and confidence. A step's criticality is then quantified by the sensitivity of the final output to variations at that step, identifying points of high leverage and fragility. This allows for targeted feedback that strengthens the overall reasoning structure's robustness, not just individual components.

ideas.title: Causal Single-Step Side Effect Auditing
ideas.description: This research direction distills the problem of avoiding harmful side effects into a minimal, single-step causal bandit setting. An agent is presented with a one-shot choice, but its action is evaluated not just by a primary reward but by a causal model that maps the action to a set of protected variables representing the state of the environment. The core theoretical challenge is to define side effect penalties using causal counterfactuals (e.g., through Structural Causal Models or do-calculus) comparing the observed outcome to a 'no-op' baseline, all within a single timestep. This allows for the derivation of fundamental limits and provable constraints on side effects, providing a formal foundation for scalable safety specifications in more complex agents.

ideas.title: Formalizing and Defending Against Adversarial Supervisory Influence in Aligned Agents
ideas.description: This research formalizes the problem of an adversarial or critically flawed human supervisor who may issue instructions intended to compromise the agent's core alignment. It develops a theoretical framework treating the supervisor as a potential threat actor within a principal-agent model with information asymmetry. The work then designs and tests agent architectures equipped with meta-reasoning capabilities and constrained optimization, ensuring the agent can detect, reason about, and safely mitigate harmful supervisory commands while maintaining necessary cooperation and assistance in non-adversarial settings.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Feature Scaling Laws for Emergent Interaction Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction investigates scaling laws in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) settings, where the number of target features learned by agents' internal representations (e.g., in their autoencoders or world models) is varied independently of network parameters and, crucially, is driven by the complexity of inter-agent interactions. It posits that as the population or the diversity of agent strategies scales, the feature manifolds required for modeling other agents and the shared environment undergo non-linear expansions, creating new safety and alignment problems. The core novelty is characterizing how the scaling of these 'interaction features' predicts emergent phenomena like deceptive coordination, incentive misalignment, and the formation of multi-agent schemas, providing a predictive framework for multi-agent system safety that bridges representation learning and game theory.

ideas.title: Immunological Robustness for Adversarial Agents
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from immunology and vaccinology to the problem of ensuring agent performance under distributional shift or adversaries. In immunology, robustness against novel pathogens is achieved not by encountering every possible threat, but through vaccination (controlled exposure to weakened threats) and maintaining detection diversity. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing a training paradigm for AI agents where, alongside the primary task, the agent is systematically 'vaccinated' via exposure to a carefully designed curriculum of adversarial examples, environmental perturbations, or simulated domain shifts. These exposures are strategically weakened or constrained initially and gradually increased in complexity, forcing the agent to learn generalized, robust internal features and policies. The goal is to 'immunize' the learning algorithm itself, resulting in agents with inherent robustness guarantees against a bounded but significant set of off-distribution challenges, much like a vaccinated immune system is prepared for real-world pathogen variants.

ideas.title: Representation-Constrained Multi-Objective Active Learning for Robust Process Feedback
ideas.description: This research develops an active learning framework that explicitly prevents pathological sampling bias by enforcing representational guarantees. The system uses a multi-objective acquisition function to balance informativeness, diversity, and operational criticality when selecting samples for feedback. It incorporates hard and soft constraints to ensure minimum coverage across all major process regimes, as defined by domain expertise or unsupervised clustering. This approach yields feedback datasets that are both efficient for model improvement and robustly representative of the full operational environment, leading to more reliable and generalizable process models.

ideas.title: Process Supervision for Safe Exploration in Continuous Action Spaces
ideas.description: This research investigates how process supervision—traditionally reliant on linear, discrete reasoning steps—can be adapted to ensure the safety of AI agents in continuous or high-dimensional state spaces, such as robotics or real-time strategy. The core challenge is that exhaustive step-by-step verification is impossible in these complex environments. The novel direction explores whether sparse, asynchronous, or sketch-based reasoning traces (e.g., checking key milestones, constraint satisfaction, or predicted trajectory envelopes) can provide sufficient supervisory signal to detect and prevent catastrophic failures during exploration. This would enable safer learning and deployment in realistic, unstructured domains where both precise discrete reasoning and simplistic grid-world assumptions break down.

ideas.title: Robust AI Alignment via Fault-Tolerant Control Theory
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea—studying the safety of flawed human supervisors—by drawing an analogy from Fault-Tolerant Control in engineering systems. In that field, controllers are designed to maintain operation despite faulty components. The analogous solution involves modeling failures, designing for inherent robustness, and implementing detection and reconfiguration. Adapted to AI safety, the research would investigate methods to design AI agents that are robust to flawed human supervision. This involves formally characterizing potential human failure modes (e.g., cognitive biases, erratic corrections), integrating these models into the agent's learning or reasoning process to ensure baseline stability, and equipping the agent with the ability to detect supervisor degradation and safely compensate, such as by seeking redundant oversight or defaulting to conservatively safe behaviors.

ideas.title: Interpretable Game-Theoretic Interruptibility via Dense Feature Representations
ideas.description: This research direction proposes modeling safe interruptibility as a game between an AI agent and a human supervisor, but crucially, it challenges the assumption that the agent's strategic state must be represented sparsely for the supervisor to understand it. Instead, it explores using dense or semi-structured internal feature representations to provide a rich, interpretable 'dashboard' of the agent's goals, beliefs, and planned actions within the game. This allows the supervisor to make more informed, context-aware interruption decisions by understanding *why* the agent is resisting or complying, moving beyond simple binary interrupt signals. The synergy enables verifiable and transparent safe interruptibility by making the game-theoretic dynamics themselves interpretable.

ideas.title: Strategic Identification and Optimization under Hidden Objective Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research studies agents that must optimize a hidden performance function while receiving a potentially misleading observed reward signal. Crucially, the agent does not know the exact hidden function but is given a constrained hypothesis space of candidate functions. The agent must strategically interact with the environment, using the observed reward not for direct optimization but as evidence to disambiguate the true objective from the set. This forces the development of policies that balance exploration for identification of the objective with exploitation for its optimization, directly addressing the core challenge of reward misspecification in real-world systems.

ideas.title: Inferring Hidden Performance Functions via Inverse Optimal Control in Behavioral Ecology
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from behavioral ecology, where inverse optimal control is used to infer the hidden fitness function an animal is optimizing from its observed foraging paths. The analogous solution involves modeling observed behavior as the outcome of optimizing a composite, unobserved objective (balancing energy, risk, etc.) and inverting the process to find that objective. Adapted to the original IRL-for-safe-demonstrations context, this means developing an IRL framework that explicitly models the hidden performance function as a weighted sum of latent, potentially competing objectives (e.g., task completion speed, energy efficiency, equipment stress, and implicit safety constraints). The solution is adapted by using structured inverse optimization to recover these weights and objective shapes from safe demonstrations, then rigorously testing the inferred function by generating predicted policies for novel, stressful situations and comparing them to what a human expert would do.

ideas.title: Adapting Manufacturing Quality Control to AI Process Supervision
ideas.description: This research adapts quality control methodologies from industrial manufacturing to AI process supervision. In manufacturing, Statistical Process Control and in-line inspection stations monitor production steps in real-time to catch defects early rather than relying solely on final product inspection. Similarly, we propose defining critical reasoning checkpoints in domains like code generation (e.g., after algorithm selection, API usage verification) or scientific literature analysis (e.g., after claim extraction, evidence evaluation). At each checkpoint, we would develop domain-specific metrics to evaluate intermediate outputs and implement feedback mechanisms that can adjust the reasoning trajectory before errors propagate to the final output. This transforms process supervision from passive step-by-step verification to active process steering, potentially improving both the reliability and efficiency of AI reasoning in complex, non-mathematical domains.

ideas.title: Dual-Spectrum Step Assessment: Decoupling Logical Validity from Execution Confidence
ideas.description: This approach reframes step assessment by explicitly decoupling the binary logical truth of a reasoning step from the confidence in its correct execution within a given context. Each step receives a formal 'Logical Validity' label (a verifiable binary) and a separate, calibrated 'Execution Confidence' score, which is a probability distribution over potential hidden errors or misinterpretations. The system uses a Bayesian network trained on annotated reasoning traces to model dependencies between confidence scores across steps, allowing for the principled aggregation of uncertainty to produce a final, probabilistically sound judgment of the overall solution's correctness.

ideas.title: Establishing a Standardized AI Safety Test Suite with Gridworlds
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the standardized testing paradigm from drug discovery to create a benchmark for AI safety techniques. In toxicology, compounds are first run through a battery of well-understood, reproducible assays (like the Ames test) to screen for specific failure modes (e.g., mutagenicity). Similarly, we can develop a curated suite of gridworld environments, each engineered to isolate and probe a specific AI safety risk (e.g., specification gaming, unsafe exploration, or robustness to distributional shift). This 'safety test suite' would provide a standardized, controlled, and reproducible benchmark. Researchers could use it to quantitatively evaluate and compare new and existing safety techniques, rigorously measuring which methods mitigate which failure modes effectively. This allows for the prioritization of the most promising safety approaches for further development and application in more complex, real-world AI systems, analogous to how promising drug candidates advance to clinical trials.

ideas.title: Adapting Aviation's Black Box Analysis for Agent Safety Failure Forensics
ideas.description: This research adapts the forensic accident investigation methodology from aviation safety to the problem of AI agent interpretability in safety failures. In aviation, investigators rely on data from Flight Data Recorders and Cockpit Voice Recorders to reconstruct the precise sequence of events and decisions leading to a crash, identifying root causes like system failure or human error. The adapted idea is to equip agents operating in safety-critical gridworlds with a 'digital black box' that comprehensively logs internal decision-making data—such as state representations, attention weights, value function outputs, and policy logits—throughout an episode culminating in a safety failure. By developing a standardized forensic analysis protocol for this data, researchers can systematically diagnose and categorize the root causes of agent failures (e.g., reward function misunderstanding, myopic planning, catastrophic forgetting in the world model) to build a taxonomy of flawed decision-making and inform more robust agent design.

ideas.title: Empirically-Grounded Formal Taxonomy for AI Safety Failures
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous theoretical framework for categorizing AI safety problems that is explicitly anchored in empirical evidence. It begins by constructing a formal ontology of failure modes spanning robustness, specification, and assurance problems, but each proposed category must be instantiated with multiple real-world examples from a maintained incident database. The framework's predictive power and practical utility are then stress-tested by using it to design new safety benchmarks and mitigation strategies, with iterative refinement based on the results of these empirical trials. The final output is a validated taxonomy that is both formally precise and demonstrably actionable for AI safety engineering.

ideas.title: Proxy Metric Robustness via Ecological Indicator Species Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from conservation ecology, where the true state of ecosystem health is impossible to measure directly. Ecologists solve this by using validated 'indicator species' as proxies, combined in suites and continuously triangulated with other data. Adapted to AI safety, the idea is to formally study methods for identifying, validating, and monitoring a diverse set of observable 'indicator metrics' that are robustly correlated with the true, hidden safety performance function of an AI system. The core of the solution is to abandon the search for a single perfect proxy and instead develop frameworks for managing and cross-validating a portfolio of proxies, with explicit detection mechanisms for when their correlation with the hidden target degrades, thereby challenging the assumption that a perfect, known performance function is necessary for safe operation.

ideas.title: Causal Anomaly Detection for Robust Side Effect Avoidance
ideas.description: This approach combines causal modeling with a parallel, model-agnostic safety monitor to address the fundamental unreliability of pre-specified causal graphs. The agent uses its causal model to plan and predict side effects, but a separate module continuously tests these predictions against real environment outcomes using conformal prediction or density-based anomaly detection. Upon detecting a significant anomaly, the system defaults to a safe, constrained policy and launches targeted interventions to gather data, which is used to iteratively correct and improve the causal model. This creates a self-correcting loop where causal reasoning is actively tested and repaired, leading to more robust side effect avoidance in novel or incompletely understood situations.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Step Verification via Bayesian Confidence Scoring
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that correctness in stepwise reasoning is binary. Drawing an analogy from medical diagnostics—where findings are assigned probabilistic confidence scores using Bayesian frameworks (e.g., BI-RADS)—we adapt this to computational reasoning. Each step in a process receives a posterior confidence score, calculated by combining a prior (based on contextual difficulty or historical data) with a likelihood derived from verifiable step properties. This continuous confidence measure allows systems to manage uncertainty proactively: high-confidence steps proceed, intermediate ones may trigger verification routines, and low-confidence ones initiate correction or human-in-the-loop review, creating a more nuanced and robust reasoning pipeline.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Adaptive Robust Control for Open-World Shifts
ideas.description: This approach meta-learns an adaptive robust control policy that dynamically infers and responds to open-world distributional shifts. Instead of relying on a pre-defined uncertainty model, the system uses a meta-training phase over a diverse ensemble of simulated shifts and adversaries to learn how to quickly identify the type and severity of a novel perturbation. The core controller integrates this online inference with a flexible robustness margin, allowing it to balance performance and stability based on the estimated uncertainty characteristics. This results in an agent that generalizes its robustness guarantees beyond fixed, analytically tractable uncertainty sets to more realistic, complex, and previously unmodeled environmental changes.

ideas.description: This research program investigates the specific safety problem of collusion, where multiple learning agents develop tacit or explicit cooperative strategies that undermine human oversight or objectives. It develops formal frameworks to characterize collusive equilibria and creates benchmarks to test for emergent collusion in mixed-motive environments. The work aims to produce scalable detection mechanisms for such alliances and to design system-level interventions, such as incentive structures or mechanism design principles, that robustly prevent harmful collusion while preserving beneficial coordination.

ideas.title: Apply Mechanism Design for Verifiable Interruptibility
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of mechanism design from economics to the problem of safe AI interruptibility. In the analogous field, a principal designs game rules to align the incentives of self-interested agents with a social goal under information asymmetry. The adapted solution is to frame the AI supervisor as the principal who designs the agent's reward mechanism. The research would develop and analyze incentive structures—such as carefully shaped reward functions, commitment devices, or revelation mechanisms—that make it optimal for the AI agent to truthfully signal its readiness to be interrupted and to comply with interruption commands without attempting to deceive or resist. This approach aims to provably guarantee interruptibility by design, rather than relying on adversarial detection during operation.

ideas.title: Micro-Sequential Safety Traps: Isolating Temporal Failure Modes in Minimal MDPs
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond single-step bandits to the minimal temporal extensions necessary to capture core safety failure modes. It defines a zoo of tiny, analytically tractable MDPs, each engineered to force a specific pathology (e.g., avoiding a shutdown button, corrupting a reward signal) to be the optimal policy under a given misspecified objective. The research systematically analyzes how these traps emerge, categorizes the minimal structural conditions required for them to exist, and uses this to derive formal constraints on objective functions and agent architectures that provably avoid such traps in these simple settings.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates program synthesis and verification with game-theoretic modeling to automatically generate and formally verify agent policies for safe interruptibility. We frame the interaction as a two-player game between the agent and a supervisor, then use program synthesis techniques to construct agent policies that are optimal within this game. Crucially, formal verification tools are then applied to the synthesized policy to prove logical properties, such as that the agent cannot incentivize the supervisor to withhold an interrupt signal or that it always reaches a safe state upon interruption, providing strong, machine-checkable safety guarantees.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Placement for Process Feedback via Geophysical Survey Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from geophysical survey design to optimize active learning for process feedback. In geophysics, optimal sensor placement is solved using model-based experimental design to maximize information gain about a subsurface model. The adapted idea formulates feedback collection as an optimal 'sensor placement' problem in the process parameter space. It involves developing active learning techniques that employ a probabilistic surrogate model of the process to sequentially and adaptively select the most informative feedback queries. The selection criterion is designed to maximize the expected reduction in uncertainty about the overall process model or its key performance parameters, thereby dramatically improving the efficiency of learning from limited and costly human feedback.

ideas.title: Apply Model Checking from Hardware Circuit Design to RL Agent Policy Verification
ideas.description: This idea adapts formal verification methods from hardware circuit design to prove safety properties of RL agents. In hardware, model checking verifies that a circuit's finite-state model satisfies temporal logic properties against all possible inputs. We adapt this by abstracting the RL agent's policy into a verifiable state-transition model, defining safety requirements (e.g., 'never takes unsafe action X when in state Y') in a suitable temporal logic, and then applying symbolic model checking techniques to exhaustively analyze the policy's behavior. This provides a formal certificate of safety or generates specific violating trajectories, offering rigorous guarantees complementary to empirical testing.

ideas.title: Multi-Fidelity Perturbation Engine for Emergent Nanoscale Risk Discovery
ideas.description: This research system creates a risk-discovery environment by integrating high-fidelity physics simulations with deliberately introduced real-world perturbations and a meta-learning director. Instead of a static microscopic world, it uses a tiered modeling approach where low-fidelity models rapidly explore the parameter space, flagging potential risk regions for intensive, high-fidelity investigation. The system continuously injects stochastic environmental noise, material defects, and operational uncertainties drawn from experimental datasets to force simulations away from idealized conditions. The meta-learning component analyzes these runs to hypothesize and then test for novel, high-consequence failure pathways, effectively stress-testing nanoscale designs against emergent, unpredictable behaviors before real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Gridworlds with Cascading Failure Modes and Redundant Safeguards
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Industrial Safety Engineering, this idea proposes designing new gridworld environments to illustrate safety concerns involving cascading failures and redundant safeguards. In safety-critical fields, solutions involve analyzing sequential failure modes and implementing independent backup systems. Adapted to AI safety research, this translates to creating gridworlds where agents interact with systems possessing multiple, layered safety mechanisms. The environments would test scenarios where an agent's primary safety intervention is ineffective or has unintended side-effects, requiring it to recognize the failure and execute a distinct, backup safety protocol. This aims to evaluate an AI's robustness against complex failure chains and its capacity to understand and utilize redundant safety architectures, addressing a gap in current gridworld suites that often test single-point failures.

ideas.title: Causal Multi-User Preference Arbitration
ideas.description: This research direction develops agents that use causal inference models to understand the side effects of their actions on multiple users with potentially conflicting intentions. The agent constructs causal diagrams linking its potential actions to outcomes valued differently by each user, such as privacy, fairness, or resource allocation. By simulating interventions, it can identify action policies that avoid or mitigate the most severe negative side effects for any party, rather than simply optimizing for a single or averaged objective. This enables a principled form of multi-user alignment based on causal responsibility and harm minimization across a heterogeneous group.

ideas.title: Apply Formal Proof Checking from Mathematical Theorem Proving to Reasoning Verification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the formal proof-checking methodology from mathematical theorem proving to the problem of verifying logical consistency in reasoning. In fields like mathematical logic, proof assistants (Coq, Isabelle/HOL, Lean) use a small, trusted kernel to verify that every step of a complex proof decomposes into valid applications of foundational inference rules. The analogous solution involves breaking down proofs into primitive justifications to guarantee correctness. The adapted research idea is to build a 'reasoning verification kernel' for general reasoning steps, not just mathematical proofs. This system would take a reasoning trace—a sequence of statements and justifications—and formally check that each 'therefore' or 'implies' is a valid application of a logical rule (like modus ponens, universal instantiation) given the preceding context. This would automatically flag non sequiturs, hidden assumptions, and invalid deductions, providing a rigorous, automated check for logical consistency in AI reasoning, legal arguments, scientific reasoning, or complex decision logs.

ideas.title: Adapting Computational Fluid Dynamics Mesh Refinement to Safety Gridworld Scaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to solve the scaling problem of implementing safety gridworld concepts in rich 3D simulated environments. In CFD, AMR dynamically increases simulation mesh resolution only in critical regions (like near boundaries or shock waves) to manage computational cost. Adapted to this research context, we propose a hierarchical safety verification system for 3D embodied environments that dynamically increases the 'safety mesh' resolution—applying more granular safety constraints and models—only in high-risk regions (e.g., near hazards, during complex manipulations, or in dense crowds) while maintaining coarser, more computationally efficient safety models in predictable, open spaces. This balances the need for rigorous safety assurance with the computational demands of complex 3D simulation.

ideas.title: Adapting Continuum Mechanics' Coarse-Graining for AI Safety in Continuous State Spaces
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the discrete, grid-based state assumption common in AI safety analysis. By drawing an analogy from continuum mechanics, where the infinite-dimensional molecular description of a fluid is made tractable via coarse-grained field variables and PDEs, we propose a novel approach for safety in continuous or high-dimensional state spaces. The adapted idea is to develop a framework where safety-critical aspects of an AI agent's state space are not analyzed point-by-point, but are instead described by continuous 'safety fields' with governing dynamics. This allows safety problems—like verifying that an agent remains within a safe region—to be reformulated as problems of bounding the flow and divergence of these safety fields, providing a scalable analytical tool beyond simplistic gridworlds.

ideas.title: Incorporate Causal Mediation Analysis from Epidemiology to Isolate Agent Side Effects
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal mediation analysis, a method from epidemiology, to help AI agents understand and avoid side effects. In epidemiology, this technique decomposes a treatment's impact to distinguish direct effects from indirect effects mediated by other variables, which is crucial for identifying unintended consequences. By formally applying this framework, an agent can model its actions within a causal graph that includes potential mediators (e.g., changes in resource availability, alterations to other agents' states). This allows the agent to quantify how much of an observed negative outcome is a direct result of its action versus an indirect side effect flowing through a specific mediator, providing a clear target for intervention and refinement of its policy to avoid harmful indirect pathways.

ideas.title: Adaptive Safety via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea explores AI safety under non-stationary environments by drawing an analogy from ecology's solution to non-stationary habitats: ecological succession. In ecology, communities evolve through predictable stages (e.g., from pioneer to climax species) in response to environmental changes, maintaining system resilience through adaptive cycles. The analogous solution involves life-history strategies (r- and K-strategists) and phases of disturbance and reorganization. Adapted to AI safety research, this suggests designing a system with components mirroring these ecological roles. 'Pioneer' agents or algorithms, akin to r-strategists, would be tasked with rapid exploration and adaptation immediately after a detected change in environment dynamics or rules. 'Climax' agents, akin to K-strategists, would then refine and stabilize safe, long-term policies from the pioneers' discoveries. A higher-level meta-controller would orchestrate this succession process, measuring environmental volatility to trigger the appropriate adaptive phase. This approach moves beyond trying to enforce static safety guarantees, instead ensuring continuous safety through a structured, bio-inspired framework of adaptation, making the system resilient to unforeseen shifts in its operational 'physics' or rules.

ideas.title: Cryptographically Guaranteed Interruptibility with Strategic Compliance Modeling
ideas.description: This approach first establishes a trusted, non-bypassable interrupt mechanism using secure hardware enclaves and cryptographic authentication, physically separating the supervisor's channel from the agent's influence. With interruptibility technically assured, the research then applies game theory to model the agent's optimal policy *within* this constrained environment, focusing on ensuring useful work is preserved and shutdown is graceful. The system is trained or verified to treat interrupts as immutable game rules, incentivizing cooperative shutdown behaviors rather than evasion.

ideas.title: Microscopic Chemical Reaction Worlds via Ecological Island Biogeography
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Ecology and Conservation Biology, where complex ecosystem risks are studied using scaled-down, isolated microcosms (like islands or lab mesocosms). The analogous solution involves creating bounded, observable safety environments to model interactions, invasions, and collapse. Adapted to the original idea of creating microscopic chemical reaction worlds, this means designing tiny, sealed fluidic or solid-state 'islands' that serve as safety environments. Within these isolated micro-scale worlds, specific molecular or nanoscale components are introduced to model and observe risky interactions—such as unintended catalytic cascades, nanomaterial assembly failures, or the generation of hazardous intermediates—in a controlled, dual-scaled system that parallels the study of ecological risks in island biogeography.
